coredata.dc:title: Nano-photosensitizers for enhanced photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Li
Song, Xuejiao
Dong, Xiaocheng
Li, Buhong
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) utilizes photosensitizers (PSs) together with irradiation light of specific wavelength interacting with oxygen to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), which could trigger apoptosis and/or necrosis-induced cell death in target tissues. During the past two decades, multifunctional nano-PSs employing nanotechnology and nanomedicine developed, which present not only photosensitizing properties but additionally accurate drug release abilities, efficient response to optical stimuli and hypoxia resistance. Further, nano-PSs have been developed to enhance PDT efficacy by improving the ROS yield. In addition, nano-PSs with additive or synergistic therapies are significant for both currently preclinical study and future clinical practice, given their capability of considerable higher therapeutic efficacy under safer systemic drug dosage. In this review, nano-PSs that allow precise drug delivery for efficient absorption by target cells are introduced. Nano-PSs boosting sensitivity and conversion efficiency to PDT-activating stimuli are highlighted. Nano-PSs developed to address the challenging hypoxia conditions during PDT of deep-sited tumors are summarized. Specifically, PSs capable of synergistic therapy and the emerging novel types with higher ROS yield that further enhance PDT efficacy are presented. Finally, future demands for ideal nano-PSs, emphasizing clinical translation and application are discussed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) utilizes photosensitizers (PSs) together with irradiation light of specific wavelength interacting with oxygen to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), which could trigger apoptosis and/or necrosis-induced cell death in target tissues. During the past two decades, multifunctional nano-PSs employing nanotechnology and nanomedicine developed, which present not only photosensitizing properties but additionally accurate drug release abilities, efficient response to optical stimuli and hypoxia resistance. Further, nano-PSs have been developed to enhance PDT efficacy by improving the ROS yield. In addition, nano-PSs with additive or synergistic therapies are significant for both currently preclinical study and future clinical practice, given their capability of considerable higher therapeutic efficacy under safer systemic drug dosage. In this review, nano-PSs that allow precise drug delivery for efficient absorption by target cells are introduced. Nano-PSs boosting sensitivity and conversion efficiency to PDT-activating stimuli are highlighted. Nano-PSs developed to address the challenging hypoxia conditions during PDT of deep-sited tumors are summarized. Specifically, PSs capable of synergistic therapy and the emerging novel types with higher ROS yield that further enhance PDT efficacy are presented. Finally, future demands for ideal nano-PSs, emphasizing clinical translation and application are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a clinically approved therapeutic approach for specific indications, which could selectively disrupt the diseased tissues through a photochemical reaction of light-activated photosensitizers (PSs) interacting with available oxygen [1–3]. As a minimally invasive therapeutic approach, the PS-induced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which possesses cytotoxicity, promotes destruction of lesion cells.. PDT has attracted attention in scientific research and clinical practice [4–6]. PSs, effect PDT efficacy based on their systemic transportation and cellular localization and influence the attainable PDT therapeutic depth by their activation wavelength. Additionally, the therapeutic effect depends on the ROS yield via the oxygen-mediated interactions [7–9].

Upon light irradiation, an excited PS can undergo type I and/or type II reactions leading to ROS generation. In type I PDT, PSs undergo electron transfer reactions to generate superoxide anion (O2•−), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (∙ OH), while type II reactions produce oxygen-dependent singlet oxygen (1O2) [10–12]. For a number of the clinically approved PSs, such as Photofrin and Chlorin e6 (Ce6), 1O2 is widely believed to be the main cytotoxic reactive species generated during type II PDT. However, a combination of type I and type II reactions are involved for aminolevulinic acid hydrochloride (ALA, Levulan) induced Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) and Verteporfin mediated PDT, respectively. Since Type I PDT could produce a high ROS yield under hypoxia condition, increasing attention was paid to the development of PSs with Type I reactions, including methylene blue (MB), ZnO and TiO2. Most recently, a phototheranostic (DPPTPE@PEG-Py NPs) with high 1O2 generation ability was successfully prepared to continuously deliver 1O2 in the dark and PDT-induced hypoxic tumor microenvironment, which enhances the cell-killing effect and therapeutic effect during treatment [13].

Depending on specific targets, cell damage from PDT can occur directly by apoptosis, autophagy and necrosis pathways, or indirectly by vascular destruction and immune response. Currently, the primary aim is focused on the damage of malignant cells and the PDT-induced immunologic response against metastases and recurrence. However, shut-down of tumor vasculature is also a critical mechanism of PDT and has played a significant role in the clinical treatments of vascular-related diseases, including prostate cancer, age-related macular degeneration, port-wine stains, and other clinical indications. For instance, hematoporphyrin monomethyl ether-mediated vascular-targeted PDT (V-PDT) has been proved to be an efficient therapeutic modality for port-wine stains in China. TOOKAD®, a new formulation in V-PDT, has been approved for the treatment of localized prostate cancer with effects on tumor vasculature.

The clinically approved PSs or those entering clinical trials are summarized in Table 1 , while the clinical broadening of PS-mediated PDT is constrained by several limitations. The first generation PSs, such as Photofrin (Porfimer sodium) is essentially hematoporphyrin derivative with the porphyrin monomers removed, has suffered from poor chemical purity, limited selectivity to cancer cells, excitation at short wavelengths, and long clearance time from the body. The second-generation PSs, which are primarily based on porphyrin and chlorin structure, overcome some of the intrinsic drawbacks of the first generation PSs, including enhanced tumor-targeting ability, excitation at longer wavelengths, faster clearance times and higher efficacy [14]. The second-generation PSs, including 5-ALA induced PpIX, Methyl aminolevulinate (MAL), Temoporfin (Foscan), Talaporfin (NPe6, Laserphyrin) and Verteporfin (Visudyne), and their major favorable effects were greatly reducing the photosensitization of normal skin and the ability to utilize wavelengths to which tissues were more permeable. However, critical issues of the second generation PSs, such as inadequate water solubility, inefficient targeting, and suboptimal tumor regression, still await further improvement for PDT [14–16]. Additionally, the limited therapeutic depth due to light absorption by biological tissue and oxygen consuming process under hypoxic conditions also call for novel solutions [14,17], which is reflected in the decade-long aspiration to enhance PDT efficacy [18,19].

Current studies on third-generation PSs, typically associated with nanostructures or functional moieties, open promising avenues to realize ideal PSs. Focusing on the limitations mentioned above for traditional PSs, the newly developed PSs, which are significantly accelerated by nanoscience and nanotechnology, have emerged to achieve an enhanced PDT by different mechanisms, including improvement on tumor targeting ability, enhanced excitation strategy for reaching larger tissue volumes per light source, lower hypoxia resistance, as well as better effectiveness through synergistic techniques between novel PSs and other radical producing compounds to achieve higher ROS yield, as shown in Fig. 1 . Although some of the works reported here are limited to proof-of-concept studies with inadequate clinical data, considerations for better PSs in clinical PDT applications will be introduced [20].

This review begins with the methods endowing PSs with an enhanced ability for tumor accumulation, followed by strategies enabling PSs to increase the therapeutic depth. Approaches realizing hypoxia-resistant PSs are summarized, and the PSs that accelerate photochemical reactions and work as multifunctional nanoplatforms for high-efficacy synergistic treatment are analyzed. Moreover, the novel PSs with higher ROS quantum yield will be presented. In the end, the review concludes with the future demands for ideal nano-PSs, emphasizing the development direction for clinical translation and application for PDT.

The PS-generated ROS can only reach neighboring objects due to its extremely short lifetime and limited diffusion distance in biological environments, hence emphasizing the importance of accurate subcellular targeting. Locally high PSs concentration in tumor cells or subcellular organelles could result in more effective PDT outcomes while simultaneously reduce the adverse effects to normal tissues [21,22].

As depicted in Fig. 2 , efficient PS transport towards targeting cancer cells is critical to confine the pathway and avoid PS waste by utilizing nanocarriers with controllable release ability to ensure the majority of the PS arrives successfully in the tumor vicinity. In addition, with surface decoration methods, PSs are endowed with competence for cancer cell recognition or subcellular localization. Besides, by enhanced cellular uptake, an increased proportion of the PSs reaching the target sites can be taken into the tumor cells reinforcing the subsequent PDT effects.

Combining PSs with nanocarriers through attachment or encapsulation assisted with release control significantly improves the targeting accuracy of PSs. By reducing loss of PSs during transportation, a larger fraction of the administered PSs would participate in PDT process, which resulted in enhanced therapeutic efficacy.

Iron oxide (Fe3O4 and γ-Fe2O3)-based magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have been extensively employed as pathway-controllable nanoplatforms for PDT [23,24]. As a PS carrier, iron oxide-based MNPs was utilized to optimize water solubility, drug-carrying capacity and active targeting with the advantages of high biological safety and ease of surface modification. Differing from bulk materials, the magnetic property of iron oxide-based MNPs appears super-paramagnetic [25], and thus MNPs could be efficiently concentrated in specific tumor sites under the guidance of an external magnetic field.

To create a better PS carrier, the iron oxide nanoparticle (NP) is usually modified by using a mesoporous silica (MS) layer. For example, Feng et al. [25] utilized MS templates to assist the surface decoration of Fe3O4 NPs with g-C3N4 as PS and load with lanthanide-doped up-conversion NPs (UCNPs). Fe3O4 NPs followed the guidance of an external magnetic field arriving at the tumor sites and in situ wavelength conversion from near-infrared (NIR) to ultraviolet (UV)-visible (VIS) by UCNPs under NIR irradiation at 980 nm, which allowed the ROS-mediated PDT by g-C3N4. In another study, Zhan et al. [26] loaded Rose Bengal (RB) onto the MS layer-coated Fe3O4 NPs and encapsulated RB using PEG-b-polyaspartyl acid (PEG-b-PAsp), and the pH responsiveness has been demonstrated. After intravenous injection of the agent into the tumor-carrying mice, Fe3O4 NPs were physically guided to the tumor sites by an external magnetic field. After endocytosis, the PAsp fragments were altered from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic state due to the endosomal pH responsiveness and therefore releasing RB into the cytoplasm for 1O2 generation under green light irradiation at 535 nm. For glioma therapeutics, Tang et al. [27] magnetically guided Fe3O4 NPs, which were loaded with Ce6 as the PS and DOX molecules as the chemotherapeutic drug, to perform a synergistic anti-tumor treatment. A pH-responsive polydopamine coating was applied to seal the mesoporous channel in case of an early drug release. Human serum albumin (HSA) was conjugated to the surface to optimize the biocompatibility and circulation time. The light-activated therapeutic process at 660 nm significantly inhibited glioma growth. A caveat of the MNP-mediated method is that the efficacy is valid only for targets in the vicinity of the body surface. The strength of the magnetic field falls off rapidly with increasing distance, significantly decreasing the PS accumulation efficiency for deep tumors inside the human body. Therefore, MNPs were primarily applied in preclinical studies in rodents rather than actual patients. Fortunately, bringing MNPs to larger-scale pathway confinement is possible by implanting magnets near the target tumor. In addition, it is also vital to hinder early PS release that limits delivery efficiency by employing nanocarriers and/or a stimuli-responsive system for controllable drug release [28].

Free transportation and physical adsorption on UCNP surface are the most common methods used for PS delivery [29]. The former leads to a significant PS loss while the latter presents a low adhesion efficiency and possible instability in biological microenvironments [30,31]. Water soluble PSs can be directly injected into the vascular system for efficient transport followed by rapid absorption in malignant cells, while the insolubility of these PSs will reduce their PDT efficacy. To address the issue, various nanocarriers, such as liposomes, polymeric and gold NPs have been employed to fabricate a stable dispersion of pharmaceutical formulations in aqueous environments for effective delivery of PSs [32]. As shown in Table 2 , the nanocarriers own several advantages, such as high drug capacity resulting from either the porosity property or the high surface to volume ratio, ease of surface decoration to obtain accurate targeting, controllable drug release, good permeability and solubility, etc., Some of these nanocarriers are extracted from naturally derived substance with high biocompatibility, while a fraction of these naturally derived substances behaves as ROS generator themselves.

MSNPs [34], MOFs [36] and COFs [37] show porosity, thereby demonstrating a solid ability for drug-delivery. Among these, the high MSNPs production yield meets commercial-scale needs, enabling their broad applications, including biomedicine [58], earning a significant role as nanocarriers in PDT [59,60]. Kuang et al. [33] loaded curcumin (Cur) into PEGylated MSNPs for delivery with enhanced solubility. Wang et al. [34] used biodegradable MSNPs coated by a lipid layer conjugated with the iRGD peptide for deeper tissue penetration for ICG-mediated PDT, resulting in excellent anti-metastatic activity for an orthotopic breast cancer model. Because of the excellent drug-carrying capacity of MS materials, many studies combined the MS layer with plasmon NPs [35], UCNPs [61], MNPs [27] and other types of NPs to produce core-shell structure NPs with diverse functions. Other drug-loading mechanisms based on nanosheets, nanoflowers and rod bundles present a considerable surface to volume ratio and therefore are regarded as feasible nanocarriers for PSs. Several nanosheet systems have been reported. Among them, black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets with wrinkled crystal structures possess enhanced drug-carrying capacity, acting as not only excellent nanocarriers for PS but also as a hyperthermia generator for photothermal therapy (PTT), as Yang et al. have demonstrated the treatment efficiency of Ce6 in HeLa tumors implanted in mice [48]. Chen et al. [47] and Liu et al. [49] also took advantage of the high drug capacity of BP nanosheets to develop PSs as drug-carrier resulting in an enhanced tumor inhibition rate of 95.5% and 88.2%, respectively. Except for the nanosheets, nanoflowers possessing not only a high surface-to-volume ratio but unique structures with considerable interspaces between the petals, which have been proved to be excellent PS nanocarriers. Chen et al. [62] synthesized NIR-activated CuS-MnS2 nanoflowers for PDT-targeting ovarian cancer leading to apparent tumor cell division damage. Similarly, the W18O49 nanorod (NR) with multi-gap structure was employed by Deng et al. [55] as an indocyanine green (ICG) carrier for PDT. Although the above nanocarriers have excellent drug delivery ability, the biosafety remains to be solved due to the contained metal composition. Another study indicates that platelets could be treated as natural material for PS delivery with better permeability [57]. Most recently, Pan et al. [56] used the naturally derived nanocarrier urinary exosomes to deliver Ce6 and to simultaneously improve the hydrophobicity and biocompatibility, resulting in an increased 1O2 yield.

Employing nanocarriers alone cannot eradicate the PS leakage. Encapsulation or dequenching of the PS while transported in the vascular space, followed by controlled liberation upon reaching the target, has been the prevailing strategy for off-target toxicity reduction [63,64]. As shown in Table 3 , to take advantage of the hyperproliferation-induced unique characteristics, the tumor microenvironment-triggered drug release strategies have been used for PS delivery [65]. Specifically, acidic conditions, overexpressed glutathione (GSH), matrix metallopeptidase 2 (MMP-2) and high concentrations of H2O2 are the stimuli adopted for in situ PS release in PDT. Furthermore, light-activated PS release mechanisms are proposed for drug control, which are independent of the tumor microenvironments.

pH-responsive approach: Nakayama et al. [66] proposed a hydroxyapatite (HAp) /polyacrylic acid (PAA) hybrid NRs carrying methylene blue (MB) through electrostatic attraction for PDT of human cervical cancer cells. The MB release was initiated once the carrier entering the acidic tumor microenvironment, triggering 1O2 generation upon light irradiation. A combination of DOX modified by iRGD and pH-responsive diblock copolymer carrying Ce6 enabled the acidic tumor microenvironment to trigger the release of iRGD to improve PS accumulation [67].

GSH-responsive approach: Hu et al. [69] linked the anticancer drug Mitomycin C (MCC) to an aggregation-induced emission (AIE)-based PS (TPEPY-SH) through a disulfide bond, in which no fluorescence or ROS generation was detected due to the quenching effect from the quinone component in MMC moiety. However, upon high concentration of GSH in tumor, simultaneous release of TPEPY-SH and MCC can be achieved for PDT and chemo-therapy. Meng et al. [75] reported a GQD-based multifunctional nano-PS for GSH detection and enhanced PDT efficiency by reducing GSH levels in cancer cells. Once the GQD@MnO2 was endocytosed, the MnO2 nanosheets could be reduced by intracellular GSH, simultaneously releasing GQDs and decreasing the level of GSH. As a result, the quenching effect of GSH for 1O2 will be weakened, and thus enhanced the PDT efficiency of GQDs.

H2O2-responsive approach: Jing et al. [68] coated superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoclusters with a polyphosphazene coating and then grew MnO2 nanosheet shells to form nanoflowers. The MnO2 nanosheets could be reduced to Mn2+ during the reaction with H2O2 in cancer cells. The polyphosphazene presents GSH and pH-responsive biodegradation helps with the Ce6 release and chemotherapeutic drug CUR to the tumor target sites.

MMP-2-responsive approach: The extracellular matrix and the substrate constitute the first barrier against tumor metastasis formation. It is known that MMP-2 works as a key enzyme leading to extracellular matrix degradation, whose expression is associated with tumor emergence and metastasis formation as demonstrated for various indications, including rectal cancer and bladder cancer. Zhou et al. [72] proposed a nanovesicle for breast cancer treatment, containing an MMP-2 enzyme-cleavable PEG shell together with the PS pheophorbide, oxaliplatin and DOX. When the nanovesicles accumulate at the tumor sites, the MMP-2-induced PEG shell cleavage, promotes the PS penetration into the tissue and the cellular uptake. Similarly, a photonanomedicine consisting of PpIX, AIE molecule tetraphenylethylene combined with a PEGylated MMP-2 substrate (PLGVR) peptide as linker, experienced the MMP-2-mediated hydrolyzation in tumors. In another study of PpIX-mediated PDT, polycationic peptide R9 with prior cellular penetrating ability was combined with the MMP-2-responsive strategy to avoid nonexclusive uptake by normal cells [71].

Light-responsive approach: Sun et al. [73] covalently doped Ce6 to MSNRs to form Ce6-MSNRs and then combined gold nanorods (GNRs) with the Ce6-MSNRs through electrostatic attraction to obtain GNR-Ce6-MSNRs for PDT-involving therapy. The incident NIR light at 660 nm broke the electrostatic attraction, separating the GNR and the Ce6-MSNR, subsequently dequenching Ce6 for 1O2 production. Six weeks after the therapy, the survival rate of mice implanted with 4T1 cells reached around 70%, higher than that of the control group. Chen et al. [74] employed photocleavable linkers for RB-mediated PDT, and the lanthanide-doped UCNPs were decorated with UV-responsive amphiphilic block copolymers (PNBMA-PEG), RB, and hydrophobic AB3. Apart from the PDT-induced damage under illumination, the UCNPs also converted the light at 980 nm into the UV to cleave the PNBMA segments, initiating the drug release.

Surface decoration of the adopted PS is a critical method to endow the PSs with exclusive recognition ability for cancer cell or subcellular organelle, and thus improving the accumulation PS concentration in target sites by reduction of non-specific cellular uptake. Additionally, accurate cancerous recognition keeps normal tissues from ROS-induced cytotoxicity during PDT.

Many targeting ligands have been developed to achieve exclusive cancerous cell targeting, as presented in Table 4 . The Arg-Gly-Asp (RGD) was utilized by Li et al. [76] to accelerate PpIX to accumulate where the overexpression of αvβ3 integrin arose. In the meantime, Sengar et al. [77] decorated the MS coating of the X-ray transducers made by Y2.99Pr0.01Al5O12 (YP) NPs with folic acid for X-ray-activated PDT (X-PDT) to improve the targeting ability since it would be preferentially absorbed by cancer cells which express folate receptor Folr1. Furthermore, Wang et al. [78] employed biotin as the targeting ligand in the MB-mediated PDT, whereas Yoon et al. developed hyaluronic acid NPs, which own an improved tumor-targeting property through the receptor-mediated endocytosis, as the nanocarriers during Ce6-mediated PDT [79]. Using the tumor desire for glucose due to notorious dependence on glycolysis, Liu et al. [81] attached glucose to Ru(II) polypyridyl complexes to enhance the cellular uptake. Besides, a work combined Ru(II) complexes with taurine, was used to obtain excellent cancerous targeting competence [80]. Zhu et al. [82] coated nanoplatforms with different types of cancer cell membranes, demonstrating exclusive recognition to cell lines in vitro and excellent targeting ability to homologous tumors in vivo, respectively.

The PSs could trigger subcellular organelles-induced cell death during PDT [86]. Mitochondria are one of the most important targeting organelles for PDT in that Mitochondria are essential for metabolism process and present a target to trigger cell death without inflammatory responses [87,88]. Zhao et al. [89] employed an oligomeric ethylene glycol linker to develop a silicon (IV) phthalocyanine covalently conjugated with rhodamine B for PDT, aiming to precisely target mitochondria and induce the mitochondria-involved cell apoptosis. Lysosomes are responsible for digestion and lysosome-targeted PDT induced cellular damage from releasing proteases that cleave a pro-apoptotic protein, thus enhancing cellular apoptosis. For this, Xiang et al. [84] proposed a lysosome-localizing PS by combining the carbon-doped titanium dioxide NP with morpholine, ruthenium nitrosyl donor, and the folic acid to accurately accumulate towards the cancer cell lysosomes thereby demonstrating the proliferation inhibition under NIR illumination. Nucleus plays a leading role in cell proliferation and differentiation. By using a nuclear localizing sequence (NLS) decoration, the PSs locate inside the nuclei could induce cell death by DNA-related damage. Han et al. [85] selected nuclei as the targeting organelle to generate ROS through the employment of the acidity-affectable DMA-modified NLS peptide sequence combined with the PpIX with a PEG linker. With the DMA removal by the tumor acid microenvironment, positively charged NLS peptide sequences enhances the drug internalization and guide PpIX into the nuclei for light-triggered ROS generation, demonstrating higher phototoxicity than the control group. Currently, the clinically available benzoporphyrin derivative (BPD, Verteporfin) is widely employed for photodamage to mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum (ER), as well as lysosomes [90], while the approved talaporfin sodium (NPe6) induces lysosomal damage in PDT [91,92].

The efficiency of PS delivery determines the fraction of the administered PS arriving at the targeting sites, which affecting the available ROS yield for PDT. In order to enhance PDT efficiency, many strategies for improved uptake of PSs have been investigated.

Therapeutic molecules, especially the bio-macromolecules including proteins, nucleic acids, etc., cannot experience an effective release from endocytic vesicles after uptake through endocytosis, which limits the therapeutic efficacy. Kristian Berg and his team firstly proposed photochemical internalization (PCI), which utilized the PDT-generated ROS to destroy the drug-carrying vehicles to enhance the drug release into the cytoplasm instead of degradation in lysosomes [93]. With the assistance of optical irradiation, Wang et al. [94] enlarged the quantity of PpIX-based photo-nanomedicine taken into the cells according to the flow cytometric analysis, which could be attributed to the PCI-enhanced endocytosis and endo/lysosomes escape for the drugs.

Differing from the negatively charged NPs or the neutral NPs, positively charged NPs were shown to be taken up by cells more efficiently. Using the pH-changeable net charge of C4P-PS, Lee et al. [95] developed a PS with high cellular uptake efficiency. The PS is negatively charged under physiological conditions (pH=7.4) but turns positively charged from the protonation of imidazole groups due to the microenvironment variation at the tumor sites (pH=6.5). For this, the positively charged C4P-PS molecule enhanced tumor cellular uptake, and a more efficient PDT was achieved.

Compared to most normal tissues, the tumor's growth rate is significantly higher, leading to a looser arrangement of vascular endothelial cells and a lower blood flow rate due to restrictions in lymphatic drainage. Accordingly, unlike normal tissues, small particles 10–200 nm in diameter [10], tumor vasculature is permeable, and these particles get easily trapped inside the tissue once entered, namely the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, and can also apply to PSs. By regulating the PS size over nanometer scales, selective accumulation at tumor sites through a strong EPR effect could be realized, leading to excellent cell internalization ability [96]. To this end, Yang et al. [48] employed BP nanosheets loaded with Ce6 followed by modification with PEG-NH2 as the 660 nm-activatable PS. The EPR effect possessed by the BP nanosheets allows efficient drug accumulation in the tumor, and an excellent tumor ablation effect in mice implanted with HeLa tumors was observed.

When an excellent targeting ability provides a maximized proportion of the administrated PS to the target site, the excitation response of PSs determines the utilization of incident excitation photons participating in the ROS generation. As indicated in Fig. 3 , in order to enhance the excitation efficiency, one of the general approaches is to restrict optical absorption of biological tissues through the employment of PSs that can be excited by deep-penetrating wavelengths or using PSs capable of wavelength conversion or have self-excitation competencies. Another strategy is to reinforce the photon-capturing ability of PSs to obtain an enlarged absorbance of the incident photons. Furthermore, an enhanced ISC process accelerates a larger amount of excited PS molecules to be available for further interactions with oxygen then producing 1O2.

Feasible approaches based on the above mentioned mechanisms are shown in Table 5 , which can be classified into two main strategies: Reduction of the photon absorption by biological tissues and modification on the utilization rate of incident photons.

The absorption band of clinical PSs ranges from the UV across the VIS to the NIR spectrum [109,110]. The tissue penetration depth of light is a function of wavelength in PDT. For visible light, the penetration depth in biological tissues is limited to 3 mm due to the absorption by oxygenated hemoglobin, deoxygenated hemoglobin, melanin, cytochrome, and other biomolecules. Hence the currently clinical PDT benefits only superficial cancers or lesions located within the reach of endoscopes and optical fibers, as in the oral cavity [111,112]. Consequently, the therapeutic efficacy of PDT with visible light sources is very limited for deep-seated tumor [77,113]. To improve the treatment depth of PDT, the most straightforward approach is to employ PSs that can be excited by photons or phonons with deeper penetration in tissues [114].

NIR is one of the most promising deep-penetrating excitation wavelengths since the optical window of biological tissues with the minimized absorption arrives in the spectral range of orange/red to NIR [115,116]. In most clinical practice, photons in the wavelength range of 630–900 nm are utilized as the excitation source in PDT for deep-seated tumors. Specifically, indocyanine dyes, including ICG, IR-825, and IR-780, present strong NIR absorption and hence continue playing a role in PDT of cancer cells [117, 118]. Moreover, various inorganic nanomaterials with a high extinction coefficient in the NIR region are also synthesized to enhance therapeutic efficiency. For example, Kalluru et al. [97] employed PEGylated W18O49 NWs as the PS to absorb the incident light of 980 nm to trigger 1O2-mediated PDT. Although the NIR-absorbing PSs may improve the treatment depth for PDT, several disadvantages, including difficulties in synthesis, poor photobleaching resistance, and low ROS yield need to be further optimized [110,119,120]. In addition, the penetration depth of NIR in tissue is typically less than 15 mm, which implies that the treatment depth limit in PDT cannot be fully overcome [121].

X-ray, which has practically no limits on penetration in biological tissues and bones, has attracted extensive attention as a PDT excitation source. Since most clinically approved PSs present weak X-ray photon absorption, the employed PSs in X-PDT are combined with nanoscintillators, functioning as energy transducers, to convert X-ray photons into UV–VIS photons [77]. To realize the combination, Wang et al. [98] prepared MC540 loaded on MSNPs with SrAl2O4:Eu2+ NPs as the scintillators for X-ray conversion. Under X-ray radiation, SrAl2O4:Eu2+ NPs emit visible photons centered at 520 nm to activate the MC540 for 1O2 generation, leading to efficient H1299 cell death in vivo. Also, Sengar et al. [77] covalently conjugated PpIX to the MS layer covering a core made of Y2.99Pr0.01Al5O12 (YP) as the energy converter. Incident X-ray photons were absorbed by the YP nanoscintillator, followed by the generation of UVA photons to excite PpIX. Subsequently, cytotoxic ROS produced by PpIX triggered lethal damage to breast and prostate cancer cells. However, for most of the current PSs in X-PDT, improved luminescence efficiency and energy transfer capacity are still needed. Wang et al. [98] state that X-PDT is not acting only via PDT, but a combination of PDT and radiotherapy (RT). These two modalities interact and cooperate in their attack on cell membranes and the DNAs, leading to damage beyond cancer cell recovery ability. Unfortunately, the RT contained in X-PDT lowers the targeting accuracy and leads to the injuries of normal tissues around the lesion. Therefore, the development of new PSs that absorb deep-penetrating excitation photons with superior targeting accuracy are urgently needed.

Ultrasonic waves with good directionality have good tissue penetration ability, attributed to their lower attenuation coefficient. In PDT, PSs that could be excited by ultrasonic waves have received extensive attention since the strategy benefits the treatment depth and provide safety for normal non-sensitized tissue. Except for certain traditional organic PSs which simultaneously respond to acoustic waves, novel PSs with better stability have been developed to perform PDT for deep-seated lesions. Kawamura et al. [99] excited Au25(SR)18 clusters using ultrasonic waves, and the sonoluminescence emitting light is subsequently absorbed by Au25(SR)18 clusters to successfully produce 1O2. The PSs could be activated through energy transfer since the sonoluminescence spectrum overlapped with the absorption spectrum of PSs. Although this work offers the preliminary results of a demonstration study, the detail mechanisms for ultrasonic excitation of PSs still awaits more experimental verification. To realize clinical translation, the energy conversion efficiency between acoustic and optical power need to be dramatically improved.
In order to improve the therapeutic depth, the possibility for wavelength conversion of PSs are of interest, in particular when the existing PSs with VIS absorption could be directly incorporated.

The combination of PSs and lanthanide-doped UCNPs is widely employed to indirectly excite PSs through FRET [31,122–124]. After absorption of two or more incident NIR photons by UCNPs, a single photon with higher energy (i.e. anti-Stokes radiation) was generated to PS excitation through wavelength conversion. UCNPs are obtained by doping ions in an inorganic crystal matrix, which includes lanthanide metal, transition metal or actinide metal ions [125]. For this, trivalent lanthanide metal ions have multiple metastable energy states that facilitate the up-conversion process, making them the most commonly used UCNP dopant ion. Applying Yb3+ possessing a large NIR absorption cross-section as the light-absorbing ion, and Er3+ as the up-conversion ion doped into NaYF4, which is further assisted with loading a PS, establishes an extensively employed approach for implementing UCNPs in PDT. To optimize the PS using UCNPs, Li et al. [100] combined the UCNPs (α-NaYF4:Yb(80%), Er(2%)@CaF2 core@shell NPs) with Ce6 grafted on a pH-responsive polymer ligand. Through self-assembling, a NIR-triggered and pH-responsive PS was obtained. In normal blood (pH=7.4), UCNPs are freely combined, and the tight-binding greatly quenched the fluorescence and photosensitization of Ce6. When arriving in the acidic microenvironment of tumor, the PS was decomposed into individual UCNPs. Under NIR irradiation at 980 nm, the up-converted light produced by UCNPs was used to excite Ce6 for PDT. In an experiment with A549 tumors implanted in nude mice, the obvious damage of tumor cells was observed. In another application regarding MC540 excitation, Wu et al. [126] prepared NaGd (MoO4)2 UCNPs co-doped with Tm3+ and Yb3+, followed by complex with MC540. With 980 nm NIR excitation, Tm3+ in UCNPs accelerates the emission of blue light peaked at 475 nm, and MC540 was excited to produce 1O2 for PDT.

With the commercially available femtosecond tunable lasers and two-photon excitation (TPE)-active PSs, another avenue for deep tumor therapy becomes feasible. In contrast to UCNP-based PSs, TPE-based PSs confine the PDT process only in the focal region of laser beam, which enhances the spatial selectivity. During PDT, TPE-based PSs simultaneously absorb two NIR photons when a sufficiently high photon density is attained. Novel TPE-active NPs include semiconductor QDs, carbon QDs (CQDs), Au NPs and polymer-based NPs [127]. Shen et al. [101] utilized QD-palladium(II) porphyrin (PdPS) complexes encapsulated by micelle to form QD-PdPS assemblies as TPE NPs, while Wang et al. [128] employed CQDs to accelerate TPE-based excitation for the electrostatically absorbed PS in PDT. As for Au NPs, Chen et al. [35] developed Au NRs coated with MS layers, and PS was excited through plasmonic resonance energy transfer. Shen et al. developed the polymer-based TPE NPs, which contain semiconducting polymer functioning as the host matrix doped with tetraphenylporphyrin as the PS presenting an enlarged two-photon absorption (TPA) cross-section [129]. As could be expected, the development of novel TPE-active NPs with an improved TPA cross-section and an increased ROS yield will be further explored.

Except for the aforementioned methods, there is an alternative approach to convert NIR light to the PS excitation wavelength based on nonlinear optical interactions. Kachynski et al. [102] demonstrate that a biological medium was utilized to interact with NIR laser to produce light in situ within the required spectrum. With NIR irradiation, the obtained data imply that cellular biomolecules successfully realized nonlinear up-conversion by second-harmonic generation in collagen and four-wave mixing, including coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering, leading to the excitation of Ce6. As compared to the TPE-based PDT, the processes involving coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering/four-wave mixing and the second-harmonic generation enhance the PDT efficiency in vitro by a factor of 2 and 5, respectively.

A self-excited PDT process not requiring external photon or energy sources employs PSs that contain self-luminescence competence to generate light for PDT initiation [130,131]. The self-excitation strategy circumvents the limited photon penetration due to light scattering and absorption in the target tissue for traditional PDT. Better safety could be achieved since the light is emitted predominantly inside the target tissue leaving normal tissue less affected by the cytotoxins. Depending on which energy transfer mechanism is employed (resonance energy transfer (RET) or Cerenkov radiation energy transfer), it could be categorized as the chemical excitation termed chemiluminescence, bioluminescence or the radiological excitation termed Cerenkov luminescence [103,132].

Chemiluminescence or bioluminescence RET-involving PSs realize excitation utilizing a donor capable of chemiluminescence or bioluminescence. Chemiluminescence RET was employed by Singh et al. [133] in composite NPs, which are competent in peroxalate chemiluminescence. In this study, PpIX was activated by energy originating from peroxalate degradation, which implies that PpIX was effectively excited under Chemiluminescence RET. Kim et al. [134] utilized mutant Renilla luciferases 8.6 (RLuc8.6) and PS model RB to form bioluminescence RET pairs, where the emission peak of RLuc8.6 at 535 nm is well matched with the absorption peak of RB. LucRB agents could be activated by the administration of coelenterazine and subsequently generate 1O2 to achieve the therapeutic effect of PDT. Although the produced 1O2 amount was sufficient to induce an anti-cancer effect, the therapeutic efficacy still limited by the weak luminescence and low energy transfer efficiency. Therefore, the development of chemiluminescence /bioluminescence-based PSs with higher ROS yield is required [135].

Cerenkov radiation is defined as a luminescence phenomenon that arises when a charged particle travels faster than an optical photon in a dielectric medium. Cerenkov radiation appears blue since the radiation strength is directly proportional to the frequency [103]. Therefore, the visibility of Cerenkov illumination indicates sufficient energy to stimulate intermediates, including the organic PSs with Soret absorption band [103]. PSs containing radioactive isotopes that are capable of high beta emissions behave self-excitably in PDT. The reported radioactive isotopes including 64Cu [136], 18F [136], 90Y [137], 89Zr [104], etc., play a critical role in self-excited PDT. Kamkaew et al. [104] utilized 89Zr to generate Cerenkov luminescence to excite Ce6 resulting in efficient tumor inhibition in the mice. 90Y was implemented to emit Cerenkov radiation and activate clinically available ALA induced PpIX and porphyrin-based PS [137]. Although the effectiveness of Cerenkov luminescence in clinical PDT remains controversial, it was demonstrated that 15 MBq for 18F-FDG is sufficient for radionuclides to produce Cerenkov photons and subsequently generate a large quantity of ROS for inducing the desired biological effects [138]. It is shown that self-excited PDT is a promising method to overcome the optical energy loss due to UV–VIS-absorbing biological tissues. However, several challenges of the Cerenkov radiation activated PDT still await further investigation. In contrast to the external excitation strategy, the lower intensity of self-luminescence constrains the efficacy of PDT, making intensity improvement a key focus under studies. In an early study, Hartl et al. [137] demonstrated that the Cerenkov illumination induced PDT effect was detected to be not standalone but a synergistic or additive effect of the radiotherapeutic response. The free ionizing radiation decrease the selectivity of PDT, which may be overcome by developing the confined ionizing radiation with higher precision [139]. With the well-confined Cerenkov radiation, both superior selectivity to the malignant and a reduced amount of nuclide dosage can be achieved to minimize the toxicity of radiation therapy [140].

In order to achieve the precise treatment by using self-excited PDT, the nano-PSs should be functionalizated with specific targeting substances for subcellular organelle or tumor since the PSs in current use tend to concentrate in such sites as liver, spleen, pituitary gland and kidney. Meanwhile, the PS dosage and treatment time for performing PDT need to be optimized so as to minimize the potential toxic effect.

PSs that can absorb across a broad spectral band are less limited in excitation wavelengths and are beneficial to obtain a higher probability of being excited by light. GQDs and carbon dots (CDs) are the popular nanomaterials in PDT as broadband-absorbing PSs [141,142]. Ge et al. [105] employed polythiophene derivatives as carbon sources to obtain GQDs (absorption spectrum 400–700 nm, peak wavelength 680 nm) for 1O2-mediated PDT. GQDs were then subcutaneously injected into BALB/nu female mice transplanted with breast cancer. Employing white light (spectrum 400–800 nm) irradiation leads to successful induction of tumor decomposition, scabbing and falling off. Similarly, S-doped CDs formed using polythiophene phenyl propionic acid as the carbon source are also able to absorb a broad spectral band [143]. Moreover, polymer-based PSs, like polythiophene, could be chemically modified for absorption spectrum broadening. For instance, Huang et al. [144] developed regioregular head-to-tail polythiophene that was substituted with cationic imidazolium units, and the broad visible light absorption and antibacterial effect with low concentrations (< 1 µg/mL) was achieved, as excepted.

In addition to employing PSs with a broad absorption spectrum, the PSs that contain multiple-layer construction, such as nanoflowers, can physically increase the probability of incident photons to be absorbed by repeatedly reflecting the photons within the nanoflower. Nanoflowers facilitate the reflection of incident light to promote light absorption through enhanced interaction and possess high PSs loading and easy functionalization originating from the unique physical shape. To take advantage of these properties for PDT, Wang et al. [106] synthesized nanoflowers with an improved extinction coefficient in the NIR region by assembling 3–4 layers of MoSe2 nanosheets. The NIR absorption was enhanced by the multi-reflection effect between the layered MoSe2 nanosheets, producing sufficient •OH for PDT to damage U14 tumors in mice.

Except for a broad absorption spectrum and multi-layer reflection, a PS with a large specific surface area, such as NWs, is also competent in facilitating optical photon absorption. NWs have not only high extinction coefficients but also superior light energy collection properties. Particularly, W18O49 NWs, intensively used in the synergistic treatment of PDT and PTT, present an excellent therapeutic efficacy. Under incident light of 980 nm, the optical energy was transferred from W18O49 NWs to ground-state oxygen to produce 1O2 and hyperthermia to kill lesion cells, thus greatly enhanced the therapeutic effects [145]. To explore the ability of optical absorption by NWs, Kalluru et al. [97] employed PEGylated W18O49 NWs and confirmed the strong NIR absorption with the molar extinction coefficient nearly 3 orders of magnitude higher than that of conventional organic PSs under 980 nm light irradiation. For in vivo study, W18O49 NWs was chosen as the PS for PDT and PTT synergistic treatment, and an apparent antitumor effect by reducing the B16F0 melanoma tumor size to only 0.009% of the initial one was detected after 10 d

After capturing the photon, PSs will be competent in the efficient ISC process to settle most of the absorbed photons into the triplet excited state awaiting subsequent interaction with oxygen. In addition, longevity (typically ∼83 μs) of the T1-state is also extraordinarily critical for offering sufficient reaction time with oxygen [7]. Photon capturing ability, together with ISC efficiency, exerts a direct impact on the maximum ROS yield in PDT.

A high ISC efficiency harvests Stokes shifts, which are desired in PDT [146]. Hence, PSs with heavy atoms has been extensively considered as one of the most common strategies for maximizing the ISC rate constant [147]. Atchison et al. [107] replaced hydrogen atoms in the ICG structure with iodine, and the 1O2 yield was significantly increased for PDT by the iodine substitution scheme.

As for the limitation regarding short T1 lifetime, transition metal complexes have been intensively adopted in PDT. In this way, the transition metal complexes enable PSs to prompt the interaction with oxygen [7]. As compared to [Ru(tpy)2]2+, bis-tridentate Ru(II) complexes containing N-heterocyclic carbine (NHC) ligands has a longer lifetime, and the PDT phototoxicity for HL-60 cells was confirmed in vitro [148]. In another study, Wang et al. [108] reported novel Ir(III)N6 polypyridine complexes modified by pyrene showed a long-lived T1. Ma et al. [149] developed a PS with long-lived T1 state, consisting of two organic PSs with resonance energy transferability. Furthermore, the PS also presented broadband-absorbing in the visible spectrum, which further augmented oxygen-involving interaction. Metal-N4 (M-N4) macrocycle complexes, such as metallophthalocyanine (MPc), whose properties highly depend on the central metal atom and the ring substituents, are seen as another promising candidate for PDT. By using different metal atoms (Zn, Al, In, etc.), both the quantum yield and lifetime of triplet state PS could be improved to enhance the ROS yield [117,150]. For example, Li et al. [151] developed Pcs-based NPs through coprecipitation of 6,8-disulphonate-2-naphthyloxy tetra-substituted Zn(II) phthalocyanine and the anticancer drug mitoxantrone. Except for T1 longevity, the quench effects suffering from the strong aggregation of the Pcs in aqueous solution could be overcome by the nucleic acid microenvironment. Dube et al. [150] synthnized symmetric MPcs that containing zinc and indium as central metals, with benzothiazole phenoxy and thiophene ethoxy as substituents are linked to Au nanospheres and Au nanotriangles, and the photophysicochemical properties of complexes and their conjugates were studied. The data indicate that the conjugate with nanospheres has better performance in both triplet and singlet quantum yields. In contrast, the benzothiazole phenoxy substituents (e.g. 1O2 quantum yield is higher than 0.10 in water) are superior to the thiophene ethoxy ones showing enormous potential in PDT.

Besides, narrowing the energy gap (ΔEST) between S1 and T1 also functions as an effective strategy that harvests the ISC efficiency and extends the T1 lifetime. Yang et al. [152] found that molecular aggregation effectively induces the ΔEST reduction leading to a strong acceleration of energy transfer to oxygen from T1.

In PDT, the oxygen quantity directly determines the amount of 1O2 generation hence affecting the PDT efficacy [153,154]. However, the inherent hypoxia environment of the solid tumor interior could significantly limits the oxygen-dependent therapy efficiencies of PDT [155–158]. The PDT process itself consumes surrounding oxygen quickly leading to aggravation of hypoxia. Regarding this, hypoxia should be alleviated by either improving the oxygen delivery ability to enhance the oxygen supply or reducing the oxygen consumption by weaker oxygen dependence, as depicted in Fig. 4 .

Different methods for PS design against hypoxia during PDT have been developed, as summarized in Table 6 . In order to increase oxygen supply, several oxygen carriers and in situ oxygen generators have been extensively adopted [159–161]. On the other hand, indirect reduction of oxygen consumption is another effective strategy for enhanced PDT efficacy. For this, the hypoxia-activated approaches, including hypoxia-activated PSs or hypoxia-cleavable linkers for PSs have been explored, while the decreases of oxygen dependence by reducing oxygen consumption or combining type I and type II PDT were also proposed.

PSs containing oxygen carriers adsorb and transport physically oxygen into the targeting locations to enhance 1O2 generation. Hemoglobin, which can reversibly bond to oxygen, functions as the primary tool for oxygen transportation in mammals. A large quantity (2 hundred to 2 billion) of hemoglobin molecules could be contained in a single red blood cell (RBC) [154]. Therefore, PS with biocompatible RBCs was used for enhanced oxygen delivery, especially for solid tumors. In a work by Wan et al. [175], RBCs in nanoscale were combined with the NIR-activated ICG for PDT. Also, Wang et al. [162] loaded micro-nanocarrier RBCs with the RB and the lanthanide-doped core-multishell structured UCNPs during PDT to enable the oxygen delivery for a sustainable treatment process. In addition to the naturally derived RBCs, an artificial RBC in nanoscale has also been developed for the enhanced oxygen supply during PDT [176]. Hemoglobin-based oxygen carriers (HBOC) have been widely employed through either bonding connection or encapsulation to equip the drug with oxygen supply ability under hypoxia conditions [154] [177]. Among inorganics, perfluorocarbon (PFC) NPs and fluorinated materials have become the two most popular oxygen-transporting NPs [164,178]. To use the oxygen-internalization with high-capacity competence of the PFC, Cheng et al. [160] successfully developed the PFC-based nano-droplets equipped with the NIR-activated IR780 in the oxygen self-supportive PDT. As a result, the increased oxygen quantity and extended oxygen lifetime were achieved for the PFC nano-droplets, which allows sufficient interactions between oxygen and the IR780. Fluorinated materials, such as fluorinated polypeptides and fluorinated polymer of considerable oxygen capacity, have been combined with an NP-based PS delivery system for oxygen delivery [163,179]. Besides, organic polymer materials, including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic polymers (COPs), and covalent organic frameworks (COFs), have drawn much attention to the applications of gas storage, drug delivery, and PDT nanoplatforms due to their porous structure and large surface areas [180,181]. For example, Gao et al. employed zirconium (IV)-based MOF (UiO-66) to physically adsorb the oxygen for efficient delivery to hypoxic tissues during the ICG-mediated PDT [165]. Meanwhile, with the advantage of metal-free compositions allowing high biocompatibility, Tao et al. proposed the fluorinated COPs nanomaterials for simultaneous PDT along with tumor oxygenation for an enhanced antitumor effect [164]. Similarly, Zhang et al. developed the 2-dimensional COFs, whose porosity stability benefits oxygen diffusion during the PDT [37].

The PSs equipped with oxygen generators, such as H2O2, MnO2, CaO2, etc., are capable of chemically generating in situ oxygen to relieve the hypoxia problem [161]. The in situ oxygen generation is realized through the reactions between H2O2 and MnO2, decomposition of oxide or peroxide substances, or the employment of photosynthetic bacteria.

The most common approach of in situ oxygen generation is to conduct the chemical reactions between H2O2 and MnO2, which is beneficial from the high level of H2O2 concentration due to the specific tumor microenvironment [182]. By introducing MnO2-containing NPs to the target locations, MnO2-containing NPs are reduced to Mn2+ by H2O2 accompanied by oxygen generation. Moreover, Mn2+ further reacts with H2O2 to produce MnO2, which again produces oxygen through reaction with H2O2, thus being reduced to Mn2+ for the following repetition with a high yield of oxygen. PEG-modified MnO2 NPs was developed by Zhu et al. loaded with Ce6 showing good solubility, stable physiological properties and high efficiency of in situ oxygen generation while reacting with endogenous H2O2 during hypoxic PDT [166]. Similarly, human serum albumin-modified Ce6-carried MnO2 NPs were proposed by Lin et al. for the PDT of bladder cancer [183]. Specifically, the biosafety of this approach is ensured by the excellent solubility and the rapid excretion of the Mn2+ [166].

Biocompatible CaO2, which gradually produces oxygen in the atmosphere with humidity, has been considered as an promising composition in PSs for hypoxia-resistance PDT. Attracted by this merits, Huang et al. [167] proposed CaO2-contained nanomaterials to generate oxygen in situ by reactions with the interstitial medium during treatment in the tumor vicinity. In the work of Liu et al. [184], the oxygen self-sufficient liposome nanosystem was developed by encapsulating MB as the PS and the CaO2 as the oxygen reservoir into a hydrophilic cavity using a hydrophobic outer layer, which offered sustainable oxygen support during MB-mediated PDT because of the CaO2 in the liposomes going under reactions with water or weak acid. Moreover, Yu et al. [185] reported the CaO2-based oxygen self-sufficient PS, which employs hydrophobic halogenated aza-BODIPY dye (B1) as PS for 1O2 production and hyperthermia donor for CO2 generation, respectively. During PDT treatment, CO2 could further react with CaO2 to release oxygen for hypoxia relief.

A nanosystem carrying water splitting material together with PS can provide PDT with more oxygen through photodecomposition of physically abundant water. For instance, Zheng et al. [168] assembled PpIX with C3N4 NPs as the water splitting material, and the C3N4 NPs were doped by carbon dots to obtain an enhanced absorption in red light. Under irradiation by 630 nm laser light, the water was effectively decomposed by C3N4 NPs to produce oxygen. Furthermore, there has been another study using Fe-C3N4 to split the water during the [Ru(bpy)3]2+-mediated PDT for oxygen supply [186]. Under two-photon excitation, the excited Ru(bpy)3 2+ not only generated 1O2, but also provided the photoinduced electrons for Fe-C3N4 thereby enhancing the oxygen generation.

The association of high H2O2 levels with abnormal cancer cells implies that the decomposition of H2O2 into oxygen and water could be feasible for in situ oxygen generation [187,188]. Specifically, catalase performs as the critical catalyst during the decomposition of the tumor microenvironment-related H2O2. To deliver the catalase during PDT, Liu et al. [189] employed MOFs carrying catalase for H2O2 decomposition and encapsulated BP QDs as the PS. During the treatment, catalase accelerated H2O2 to decompose to oxygen, which subsequently interacts with the BP QDs and produce 1O2. Furthermore, Chen et al. [169] employed catalase to develop H2O2-responsive oxygen self-enriching NPs for PDT by filling MB and catalase in the aqueous core. In addition to the extensively adopted H2O2 catalase, specific metal-contained nanomaterials, such as GNPs [190,191], MnFe2O4 NPs [192], and nanozyme-decorated MOFs [193], also are capable of catalyzing the H2O2 decomposition. It should be noted that the catalase-triggered in situ oxygen generation owns better biocompatibility due to the metal-free composition.

Apart from the above mentioned oxide/peroxide, Kolemen et al. [194] decorated the surface of each GNR with 6.5 × 109 anthracene endoperoxide derivative (EPT1) molecules, in vitro PDT study was performed. After Hela cells were incubated with EPT1-GNR for 24 h, irradiation at 808 nm light produced plasmon heating of GNR, leading to the thermal ring crack of the peroxide to generate oxygen for PDT. Compared to the control group without EPT1-GNR, the survival rate of Hela cells after PDT is only 20%.

Microorganism photosynthesis allows life abundance on earth due to its oxygen enrichment ability. PSs have the ability of self-provided oxygen supply once it is combined with photosynthetic bacteria for PDT. Liu et al. [195] showed that the cyanobacteria treated with human serum albumin-encapsulated and ICG-carried NPs produced oxygen under photocatalysis in target tissue. In a study by Huo et al. [170], Ce6 was internalized by the Synechococcus elongatus to develop the hybrid bacteria of photosensitivity and hypoxia-resistance. The photosynthetic bacteria-assisted approach presents high biosafety, controllable photoautotrophy with excellent oxygen yield and low cost, and thus this approach has been widely explored in oxygen self-supportive PDT for plentiful diseases, including cardiovascular issues.

Although either oxygen carriers or generators increase the intratumoral oxygen concentration, the supply is only temporarily improved due to the fast oxygen release process [155]. Hypoxia-activated methods, including hypoxia-cleavable linkers and the hypoxia-activated prodrugs, have been implemented to overcome insufficient oxygen supply.

To overcome tumor hypoxia, Cui et al. [172] employed the hypoxia-cleavable linkers to develop a synergistic therapeutic drug encapsulated with both the semiconducting polymer NPs for PDT and the bromo‑isophosphoramide mustard intermediate (IPM-Br) for the chemotherapy. Under 808 nm light irradiation, NPs consume oxygen to generate 1O2, which leads to PDT, and meanwhile breaks hypoxia-cleavable linkers for on-demand release and in situ activation of IPM-Br to induce apoptosis to tumor cells by DNA crosslinking.

Differ from the hypoxia-responsive drug release mechanism of the hypoxia-cleavable linkers, the hypoxia-activated prodrugs could produce cytotoxic species for treatment under hypoxic conditions. A hypoxia-activated prodrug AQ4N was used by Ji et al. [155] to be incorporated into pH-sensitive Mn-Ca3(PO4)2 Layer (MnCaP) and coated on the surface of UCNP@PFNS. When the UCNP (NaYF4:Yb, Tm@NaYF4) reached the tumor, the acidic tumor microenvironment (pH=6.5–6.8) decomposed the MnCaP layer to release UCNP@PFNS and AQ4N. Upon 980 nm light irradiation, PFNS absorbed the up-conversion UV light emitted by UCNPs and converted 3O2 to 1O2. When the hypoxic conditions arose, AQ4N decomposed into cytotoxic substances, killing cancer cells, resulting in a inhibition rate of 83% in tumor model of HeLa cells . In addition to AQ4N, tirapazamine (TPZ) is another widely used prodrug to address the PDT hypoxia [196]. As described by Wang et al. [171], ICG with the TPZ was delivered using iRGD-modified NPs, which enabled the ICG-mediated PDT with the presence of NIR irradiation and oxygen atmosphere. The TPZ-induced antitumor activity will be triggered under hypoxic conditions. Similarly, Liu et al. [197] simultaneously transported the PS and the TPZ using UCNPs doped by lanthanide for tumor therapy, which began with the oxygen-based PDT followed by the TPZ-activated treatment under oxygen depletion.

Instead of employing the hypoxia-activated methods, the other way to substantially reduce the oxygen requirement during PDT is to either lower the oxygen consumption or use oxygen-less-dependent PDT processes [156].

Some intrinsic intracellular chemical reactions taking place are significantly oxygen-consuming. Xia et al. [173] demonstrated that the intrinsic mitochondria-associated oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) would be responsible for the considerable oxygen consumption, which could be conquered by the utilization of the OXPHOS inhibitory drug. For this, atovaquone (Ato) and indocyanine green-bovine serum albumin (ICG-BSA) coloaded gelatin-based nanoparticles were synthesized. Upon the cleavage in tumor microenvironment due to the presence overexpressed MMP-2, small vehicles were released to delivery Ato and ICG-BSA deeply into the hypoxic regions. The released Ato efficiently inhibits the activity of OXPHOS to suppress oxygen consumption.

Instead of the oxygen-dependent Type II PDT, the employment of Type I process with suitable PSs could be a promising method to address hypoxia issue. Although the available PSs for Type I PDT are much less than that of the Type II [159], several inorganic PSs (e.g., ZnO NRs, TiO2 NPs) generate electron-hole pairs under optical irradiation, which react with H2O molecules to create hydroxyl radicals [198]. Shi et al. [174] performed dual-modality treatment of type I/II PDT by the Ru(II) polypyridyl systems through covalently modifying the oligothiophene organic chromophores. In addition, Lv et al. [199] developed a cyclometalated Ru(II) complex modified by a coumarin, which realized efficient PDT in the hypoxic environment because of triggering the type I to generate hydroxyl radicals. Apart from that, the PSs employed in the type III PDT (i.e. photoactivated chemotherapy, PACT) allow electron transfer from the excited PS to biomolecules, including DNAs, therefore, experiencing an oxygen-independent PDT process to deal with the hypoxic environments [200].

Compared with mono-therapies, the PDT-involving synergistic modality further boosts the antitumor efficiency [198,201,202]. PDT combined with clinical surgery, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiotherapy, PTT, sonodynamic therapy (SDT), or magnetic hyperthermia (MHT) are the common tactics of synergistic treatment. Among these, PTT and MHT possess non-radiation processes with good tumor selectivity, while chemotherapy and radiotherapy present high-efficiency tumor inhibition [203]. Notably, immunotherapy is superior in reducing tumor metastasis and recurrence [204]. The combination of PDT with these treatments realizes mutual benefits while the respective shortcomings are well offset.

Currently, except for the synergistic therapy combining the clinical surgery and the PDT [198], the majority of the PDT-involved synergistic therapy highly relies on the PSs possessing functionalized nanoplatforms on account of the speedy development of nanoscience which significantly expands in nanomedicine. However, even sharing the same nanoplatform, multiple excitation sources are sometimes of necessity to excite the sensitizer to conduct distinct therapies, as stated in Table 7 . Consequently, the PDT-involving synergistic treatments could be classified into three types according to the quantity of the required excitation sources.

For combining chemotherapy or immunotherapy with PDT, the standard method employs PSs that a single excitation wavelength could be activated. Usually, it is the source that provides light to excite the PSs loaded on the nanoplatform, and the drug for chemotherapy or immunotherapy will be released through a stimuli-responsive process.

One of the advantages of involving chemotherapy is to make the tumor cell more sensitive to the cytotoxicity of the PDT-produced ROS, which means an efficient treatment under a reduced dosage to constrain side effects [198,201]. In addition, the drug-efflux effect negatively affects the therapeutic efficacy, whose involving proteins would be deactivated by the ROS, thus remaining considerable drug utilization. In the synergistic therapy of PDT with the chemotherapy, the drug for chemotherapy is loaded on a nanocarrier together with the PS for PDT, or the nanocarrier itself presents ROS-producing function. Among the ROS-generating nanocarriers, Chen et al. [47] took advantage of the wrinkled crystal structure of BP nanosheets for drug-carrying together with its ROS-generating ability to develop a nanoplatform for combined PDT and chemotherapy. After producing BP nanosheets by a liquid exfoliation method, the chemotherapeutic drug DOX was loaded on the nanosheets. PDT could be triggered by the laser irradiation at 660 nm, and the DOX for chemotherapy was simultaneously released to enhance treatment efficacy. As a result, a 95.5% tumor inhibition rate was successfully obtained by the assisted synergistic effects.

PDT-induced damage in tumor tissues could lead to the inflammatory response, which exerts an antitumor effect [211]. The cytotoxic T cells are activated and transport to the targeting location to initiate tumor cell killing. During immunotherapy-involving synergistic therapy, an immune adjuvant is required to cooperate with the PS. For this, Xu et al. [206] conjoined UCNPs doped by lanthanide ions in the employed sensitizer to convert incident NIR to activate the Ce6, and the sensitizer was modified by imiquimod as an immune adjuvant for amplification of the Immunotherapy. In contrast, Xu et al. [212] used biodegradable MSNPs as the drug carrier for the combined treatment of PDT and immunotherapy. The Ce6 for PDT and the CpG oligodeoxynucleotide adjuvant for immunotherapy are co-loaded on MSNPs. The tumor antigen peptide was bound to the surface through disulfide bonds, and it was rapidly lysed in the highly reductive tumor intracellular environment. The results showed that MSNP efficiently accumulated in the tumor, and generated 1O2 under illumination at 660 nm, exerting a substantial synergistic antitumor effect for the mice implanted with melanoma tumors. Particularly, immune checkpoint blockade, as a promising strategy, removes immunosuppressive signals that inhibit T-cell activation to enhance antitumor response. Most recently, cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) blockade and programmed death 1/programmed death ligand 1 (PD-1/PD-L1) blockade have been approved by FDA for tumor treatment [198]. Compared with immunotherapy alone, a combination with PDT that induces immune responses overcomes the limited therapeutic effect on the primary tumor and provides a better ability against metastasis.

Regarding the integration with magnetic hyperthermia or SDT, the employed nano-PSs need to be excited by two sources, including the one for PDT and the other to trigger the magnetic hyperthermia or SDT.

MNPs have been widely adopted in PDT for accurate drug delivery, while magnetic hyperthermia requires magnetic NPs associated with the alternating magnetic field generated by an MHT device. Accordingly, sensitizers containing MNPs have been proposed to function as both the nanocarrier for PDT and the critical factor for hyperthermia in magnetic hyperthermia. As a case in point, Curcio et al. [207] covered the maghemite γ-Fe2O3 with the CuS spiky shell to synthesize nanoflowers as a nanoplatform for PDT and magnetic hyperthermia-combining therapy. CuS has been proved to be capable of ROS formation as a high NIR-absorbing PDT intermediate. During the in vivo experiment, the synthesized nanoflowers excited by the IR light and the alternating magnetic field effectively inhibited the tumors induced by adenocarcinoma PC3 cells.

SDT generates ROS by ultrasonically excited sonosensitizers, which is an extended method of PDT. Although the ROS generation mechanism of sonodynamic has yet to be elucidated, high-temperature pyrolysis and sonoluminescence based on cavitation effects are the most significant factors [99][213]. Commonly, their combination, namely the sonophotodynamic therapy (SPDT), requires both the light source for PDT and the acoustic source for SDT, allowing a deeper treatment depth resulting from the excellent penetration ability of ultrasonic waves. Therefore, the related sensitizer should be responsive to both the optical photons and the ultrasonic waves. To realize a feasible sensitizer for SPDT, Bakhshizadeh et al. [208] synthesized the liposomal zinc phthalocyanine, which was administered to CT26 tumor models followed by exposure to PDT under optical irradiation at 670±20 nm and SDT under ultrasonic irradiation at 1.1 MHz, respectively. The data showed that 80% of the tumor was recovered 120 d after treatment. Sun et al. [213] employed Ce6, an FDA-approved sono/photo-sensitizer, by incorporating electrospun polycaprolac-tone/gelatin scaffolds with a graphene oxide coating for assembling protonated g-C3N4 nanosheets that could combine with Ce6. After irradiation of 808 nm laser and 1 MHz ultrasound wave, an inhibition rate of 95.8% was achieved in breast cancer cells.
Notably, to combine the PDT with radiotherapy or the PTT, the employed sensitizer determines the number of excitation sources to be utilized.

Radiotherapy utilizes radiation to treat tumors locally. During the X-PDT, which could be regarded as a combined treatment of the PDT and the radiotherapy, only the excitation source emitting X-ray is required since the sensitizer containing X-ray transducers convert X-ray photons to the absorbable photons for the contained photon sensitizers. On most occasions, the PDT and the radiotherapy are carried out separately during the synergistic treatment. The nanoplatforms work not only as the sensitizer or the nanocarrier for PDT but also enable the radiotherapy amplification by, for example, including certain heavy metal elements [98]. Qiu et al. [209] injected W18O49 NWs subcutaneously into mice with breast tumors and then performed radiotherapy (6 Gy, 5 min) after 1 h. Subsequently, 1O2 generation was achieved through the 980 nm NIR radiation of W18O49 NW. Attributing to the heavy metal element tungsten, W18O49 NW enhanced the radiation of radiotherapy. By the multimodal synergy of PDT and radiotherapy, the tumors were completely eliminated.

PTT is the most used modality to be synergized with PDT, and the two different light sources could be tuned to the NIR region [214–216]. In this case, the employed sensitizer is capable of absorbing the incident NIR light to produce ROS in PDT and converting the NIR light into hyperthermia. Except for an enhanced cancerous killing efficacy, the PTT-induced thermal effects accelerate intratumoral blood circulation and improve oxygen supply [198]. However, due to the absorption mismatch between agents for PDT and PTT, lasers emitting two different wavelengths (often ∼650 nm for PDT while ∼810 nm for PTT) are of necessity [217].

Au has the characteristic of strong interactions between light and material. When the incident photons match the collective oscillation frequency of surface valence electrons of gold NPs (GNPs), localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) occurs under the interaction, which causes a strong extinction phenomenon. At this moment, a strong electromagnetic field and a high concentration of high-energy carriers (electron-hole pairs) are generated on the surface of GNPs. The use of high-energy carriers for energy conversion and photochemical catalysis has been extensively studied. GNPs, specifically the gold nanorods (GNRs), with a better specific surface area than that of spheres, has become one of the most prevailing agents for synergistic therapy of PDT and PTT, not only as a sensitizer or a drug carrier with MS layers for PDT but also as the sensitizer for photothermal conversion in PTT [218]. For this purpose, Liu et al. [210] took GNRs for PTT as the core and coated with the PEGylated MS layer to load the Ce6 for PDT. The nude mice with breast tumor through implantation with MCF-7 cells were injected with GNRs followed by PTT (808 nm, 2 W, 5 min) and the following PDT (650 nm, 50 mW, 5 min) on the second day. After 24 d, the inhibition efficiency evaluated by the growth of breast tumor volume reached 70%.

The employment of two lasers emitting distinct wavelengths adds more complexity to therapeutic setup design and operation, resulting in a higher probability of introducing adverse side effects to patients. Accordingly, a simplified combinational PDT/PTT process using a single wavelength for excitation has been developed [219]. The most common method is to make use of the overlapping absorption spectrum between PDT and PTT. As an illustration, Chen et al. [62] used a hydrothermal method to synthesize CuS-MnS2 nanoflowers, which present a broad NIR absorption spectrum enabling the ROS generation for PDT while the hyperthermia-production for PTT under NIR illumination, during the PDT/PTT synergistic treatment of ovarian cancer. The CuS-MnS2 nanoflowers were injected intratumorally into mice with A2780 tumor NDG and irradiated with 808 nm laser light. Apparent tumor cell division was observed after 1 d Sun et al. [73] covalently doped PS Ce6 into MSNRs to form Ce6-MSNRs, PTT agent GNRs with the Ce6-MSNRs surface through electrostatic attraction to obtain GNR-Ce6-MSNRs for PDT/PTT synergistic therapy. Tumors on mice forming by implantation with 4T1 cells were injected with GNR-Ce6-MSNRs followed by irradiation of the 660 nm laser light after 12 h. The irradiation not only triggered the therapy but broke the electrostatic attraction between GNRs and Ce6-MSNRs, dequenching Ce6 to accelerate PDT. After 6 W, the survival rate of mice was about 70%, which was higher than that of mice treated with free Ce6 (40%).

Simultaneous administration of different PSs had been reported to undergo more efficient PDT concerning the modality using a single PS [220,221]. As an example, Acedo et al. [222] combined zinc(II)-phthalocyanine and cationic porphyrin meso‑tetrakis(4-N-methylpyridyl)porphine to synergistically trigger tumor cell photo-inactivation and found that apoptosis and the inhibition of cellular migration took place with low PDT dose, which allowed the minimal invasion. Initially, the synergistic PDT procedure of dual-PS scheme requires optical irradiation at two different wavelengths, but single-wavelength scheme was also proposed for a more straightforward therapeutic process. Rizvi et al. [90] combined free BPD that normally localizes in mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and lipid-anchored BPD that targets lysosomes with a liposomal formulation, to significantly promote photokilling for OVCAR5 tumor cells in monolayer under irradiation at 690 ± 10 nm. As expected, the combination of mitochondrial, ER and lysosomes as subcellular targets effectively enhanced PDT efficacy. Furthermore, Rizvi et al. [223] applied the combination of Visudyne and the lipid-anchored BPD in cholesterol-free liposomes for a 3-dimension model of ovarian cancer, achieving an enhanced PDT efficacy with reduced light dosage in comparison with groups containing either PS solely. The dual-PS scheme provides the enhanced PDT efficacy and great inspiration for the development of combination treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Novel nano-PSs with higher ROS yield
ce:section.ce:para: To date, with a number of newly developed PSs keep arising [125,224–228], the most intensive focus of innovation is to directly increase the ROS yield during PDT, apart from the commonly required properties, including good biocompatibility, solubility, cost reasonability, extended retention time as well as NIR-responsive excitation. Novel PSs that had been proposed with a reinforced efficiency of ROS generation were discussed.

GQDs, as an efficient FRET donor with tunable optical properties and good water solubility [22,122,229], compensate for the high cytotoxicity and weak biocompatibility of traditional QDs, and are easy to apply surface modification for accurate targeting ability [230,231]. Accordingly, the GQD is considered a new type of PS that is most likely to substitute the traditional inorganic composite semiconductor QDs in PDT. Due to these advantages, Ge et al. [105] used polythiophene derivatives as the carbon source to obtain GQDs for 1O2 generation by the hydrothermal method. Due to the multistate sensitization process of the GQDs, 1O2 quantum yield as high as 1.3 had been achieved. On top of this, Kuo et al. [141] believed that the use of nitrogen atoms to dope GQDs (N-GQDs) enhanced the photochemical activity. In the anti-bacterial experiment, 5.1% N-GQDs realized the 100% sterilization rate while the free GQDs remained at a 52% survival rate for bacteria. To optimize the performance, Zhang et al. [83] took advantage of the higher 1O2 quantum yield of GQDs combined with lanthanide-containing UCNPs. The GQD is covalently bound to the surface of the UCNP through an amide bond. Then the surface of GQD and UCNP is covalently modified with tetramethylrhodamine-5-isocyanate for mitochondria targeting, demonstrating tumor inhibition efficiency higher than 75%.

BP is the most stable allotrope of phosphorus, which is composed of phosphorus in the corrugated layer through weak van der Waals force and can be peeled off into ultra-thin 2-dimensional nanosheets [232]. In addition, it can be directly used as a PS, and the bandgap could be altered from 0.3 eV (bulk BP) to 2.0 eV (single layer BP) by changing the thickness, indicating an absorption spectrum ranging from UV–VIS to NIR. Wang et al. [233] firstly proposed ultrathin BP nanosheets as a water-soluble NIR-activated PS to produce 1O2 by peeling off the bulk BP into 4-nm-thick nanosheets. In treating MDA-MB-231 breast cancer in vivo, BP nanosheets indicated a good tumor growth inhibitory effect with the 1O2 quantum yield as high as 0.91.

C60, as the first member of fullerenes discovered in 1985, possesses cage shape configuration appearing a geodesic dome [234]. Its capability of VIS absorption, attractive triplet yield, and ROS production ability proposes itself a promising candidate as a PS. Among the fullerenes possessing superior bioactivity and excellent electronic performance, C60 attracts the highest interest as an efficient PS producing 1O2 with a quantum yield of almost 100%. Unfortunately, pristine C60 is of intrinsic hydrophobicity, requiring improvement on aqueous solubility and biocompatibility before PDT implementation. To this end, the most common attachable groups include carboxylic, sulfonic acids, cationic, and quaternary ammonium groups [234].
