Moreover, C60 solubility would be improved by the graphene oxide [235], and the NIR-inactivity needs to be conquered through the combination with UCNPs.

Since life activities are primarily carried out in water, fluorescent materials, such as organic dyes, QDs, rare earth ion-based NPs, etc., are used for biological imaging, diagnosis and medical treatment. Specifically, most organic dyes possess a hydrophobic structure making it easy to form irregular aggregates in water leading to π-π interactions between aggregated molecules. Hence, the fluorescence intensity is significantly reduced at a high concentration or aggregation state. Attributed to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), traditional fluorescent materials can only be used in dilute solutions with low concentrations. However, the reduced fluorescent molecules make themselves more susceptible to incident optical energy, thus inducing undesired photobleaching. The ACQ phenomenon also occurs when organic dyes are doped or are encapsulated in a nanocarrier. Fortunately, AIE, in which strong fluorescence could be emitted by AIE-based PSs when the molecules aggregate, had come on the scene [236,237]. The optical energy absorbed by fluorescent molecules would be dissipated in optical emission or nonradiative ways, including heating and physical motions of molecules (i.e. rotations and vibrations). In AIE, the space of aggregated molecules is significantly confined to realize the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR), and their twisted configuration hinders π-π interactions. Meanwhile, their molecule vibration also consumes energy and requires the restriction of intramolecular vibration (RIV). Focusing on the ACQ, which results in a reduced ROS yield, PSs based on AIE had been developed with an exhibition of enhanced fluorescence and high photosensitivity to optical stimulation. The efficient ROS generation of the PSs originates from the suppression of RIR and RIV that consume the excited PSs of T1, which significantly burden the ROS yield during conventional PS-mediated PDT [238]. Particularly, luminogens with AIE characteristics (AIEgens), such as tetraphenylsilole (TPS) and tetraphenylethane (TPE), demonstrate intensive fluorescence under aggregation [239]. As one of the examples, Wu et al. [240] designed TPETCAQ NPs, which presents broad absorption spectrum and a NIR emission peaking at 820 nm, by introducing donor and acceptor units into an AIEgen, while Zheng et al. [239] synthesized a series of push-pull AIEgens through conjugation of diphenylamine group and electron-withdrawing groups demonstrating NIR fluorescence, large TPA cross-section and superior 1O2 quantum yield. However, AIE-based PSs in the aqueous solution face some issues, including short-wavelength absorption spectrum induced by rotor-shaped configuration, broad emission bandwidth originating from the existence of different emission species, as well as unstable brightness affected by the molecular packing modality, which has to be dealt with prior to its potential application in PDT [236].

The earliest preclinical applications of PDT date back to the 1900s, yet it is still in the early stage. Although considerable progress has been witnessed in PSs development, only a limited quantity gained access from bench to bedside. Ever since the first approval of Photofrin in Canada in 1993, the number of cancer-treatment PSs with clinical approval around the world is very limited. For the past two decades, different strategies have been proposed to develop multifunctional nano-PSs for enhanced PDT efficacy, which including targeting, excitation response, oxygen supply, synergistic therapy, and higher ROS yield.

Tumor-specific PS is the most straightforward approach for efficient accumulation to precisely destroy target tissue. Nanocarriers significantly improve the targeting ability of PSs through EPR effect, but the efficiency greatly depends on size, surface modification and morphology, which requires meticulous design. Additionally, nanocarriers decorated by tumor cell/subcellular organelle-targeting substances primarily modify the distribution and penetration competence of PSs. Moreover, the tumor-micro-environment-activatable or light-responsive drug release strategy of the selected nanocarrier need to be reinforced the therapeutic effect by reducing premature PS release.

To conquer the limitation of excitation light penetration, the most common method is to develop PSs that can be activated by light with longer wavelength. NIR-stimulated UCNPs attract the most attention as wavelength converters for PDT, but the tumor within 15 mm thick can be successfully treated. Internal lighting by X-ray, chemo/bioluminescence, or Cerenkov illumination could thoroughly remove the barriers of tissue penetration, establishing self-excitation a very promising lighting scheme for PDT despite the energy transfer efficiency needs improvement. Also, strong absorption, high ISC rates and long T1 lifetimes of PSs effectively enlarge the proportion of incident photons for higher ROS generation.

To overcome hypoxia of solid tumor during PDT, one of the solutions is to enhance oxygen supply through carriers or generators. However, the delivery capacity of carriers is finite, and the release procedure of generators proceeds fast. The suggested method to address the issue is to reduce oxygen dependence by combining Type I PDT reactions or hypoxia-activated therapy, which could alleviate limitations induced by hypoxic conditions.

To enhance therapeutic efficacy, the combination of PDT with other therapeutic modalities has been explored. By utilizing the advantages of individual therapy while compensating for the shortcomings, synergistic effects with mutual benefits highly profit cancer treatment. For this, PDT/PTT tactic holds the best prospect, which could be attributed to a similar excitation window at NIR and the availability of materials that can generate both 1O2 and hyperthermia. In addition, the dual-PS scheme, simultaneously targeting different subcellular organelles, not only boosts the therapeutic efficacy but also inspires the design of synergistic modality. Specifically, concerning the other synergistic modality, PDT with immunologic response has an enhanced ability against cancer metastasis and recurrence, and requires only a single excitation source presenting easier operation and simpler facility requirement.
For the newly developed PSs for higher ROS yield, AIE-based PSs prove the best candidate with enhanced ROS yield through suppression of non-radiative RIR/RIV.

To date, the development of nano-PS-mediated PDT is still in its infancy. Most of the aforementioned studies still lack convincing clinical evidence, and further investigation is required to bring nano-PSs to valid therapeutic regimens in clinical PDT. Firstly, the prolonged and sophisticated formulation procedures for nano-PSs raise labor and material costs, hindering their popularization in reality and requiring further simplification and industrialization. Secondly, insufficient biological examinations of nano-PSs call for particular attention to long-term study of the toxicology since more efforts are made to obtain phototoxic benefits for malignant cells other than concern with the invasiveness towards normal tissues. Moreover, the potential toxicity of the employed materials in nano-PSs should be seriously considered, and the fate of nano-PSs after entering human body is not yet clearly understood. Hence, the related safety issue attracts much attention.

In order to develop ideal nano-PS for clinical PDT, one possibility is to speed up the clinical translation of the available nano-PSs already under in vitro/in vivo investigations. As descripted in Fig. 5 , another increasingly attractive solution, is to directly upgrade the clinically approved PSs with the above emerging nanotechnology-based strategies. In this case, the strategies for nanocarriers with controllable drug release, targeting substances (i.e. subcellular organelle and tumor), NIR absorption or internal excitation, hypoxia-triggered antitumor drug and mutual-benefit synergistic therapy could be adopted to circumvent the limitations of the currently approved PSs. To accelerate the clinical translation of nano-PSs for PDT, it would be helpful to utilize the clinically approved materials or components existing in the human body for synthesis.

Although nano-PSs are in a start-up process, the obtained results are highly encouraging. Given the fast development of nanotechnology and nanomedicine, it is anticipating that ideal nano-PSs will come true around the corner, and the multifunctional nano-PSs will expand the PDT scope of clinical indications.
